topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office seemed too bright, the hum of the air conditioning an aggressive drone in the silence. Sarah stared at the printout, her hand trembling slightly as she traced the faded camp logo. Mark leaned over her shoulder, his usual easygoing demeanor replaced by a taut stillness. "No way," he breathed, his voice barely audible. "We went to Camp Whispering Pines? Last summer?" Sarah pulled her hand back as if burned, her gaze darting around the cubicle farm as if expecting someone to materialize from the beige partition walls. A cold prickle crawled up her spine. She felt a sudden, desperate urge to check her phone, to see if this was some elaborate, cruel joke, but her fingers remained stubbornly still on the paper. The memory, vivid and unwelcome, flooded back—the smell of pine needles and campfire smoke, the sheer, uncomplicated joy of those days. Now, the reality of the shared experience hung heavy and suffocating in the stale office air.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the details in my head, the way the sun slanted through the canopy, the taste of slightly burnt s'mores. Every time I looked up, I half-expected to see another familiar face, another echo of that distant summer. When David pointed out the identical camp name on the shared itinerary, a strange hollowness opened up in my chest. I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. My breathing hitched, shallow and fast. "That can't be," I managed, the words coming out thin and reedy. David just looked at me, his expression unreadable, a flicker of something unreadable crossing his eyes before he quickly looked away. The silence that followed felt vast, amplifying the frantic rhythm of my own pulse. I needed to move, to leave this sterile environment, but my feet felt rooted to the spot, heavy and unresponsive.

[story 3]

He didn't look up when I mentioned the shared camp, just meticulously straightened a stack of files, his movements precise and unnervingly calm. But I saw the slight tightening around his jaw, the way his shoulders subtly drew inward. It was enough. I felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in my stomach, like the floor had tilted beneath me. "You were there too?" I asked, my voice sounding strangely distant, as if I were speaking from underwater. He finally met my eyes, and for a brief second, I saw a raw vulnerability there that made my throat constrict. He offered a small, tight smile that didn't reach his eyes. The air in the room seemed to thin out, leaving me feeling exposed, as if every secret from that summer was suddenly laid bare under harsh scrutiny. I just stood there, absorbing the weight of that shared, unspoken history.
